title: Italic capitals — chancery hand
subtitle: The broad-edge capital letters of the Italian chancery tradition
excerpt: The chancery italic capitals are the correct partner for the Italic minuscule. They differ from the Foundational capitals in slope (5 to 10 degrees), in letterform (more condensed, with branching arches), and in construction (fewer strokes, with more joins). Written at a 45-degree nib angle, they carry the same visual rhythm as the Italic minuscule body.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on Italic hands and formal cursive. Internet Archive scan available. The chancery italic tradition is documented in Renaissance humanist writing manuals including Arrighi (1522), now public domain; Johnston synthesised the modern teaching version from these sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The formal italic hand developed in the Italian administrative chanceries of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, characterised by a 5 to 10 degree forward slope and a 45-degree broad-edge nib angle.
The curved branching stroke in letters such as n, h, and m, where the stem curves outward from partway up the downstroke, not from the top.
The point on a stem where the arch begins to branch outward, typically about one-third of the way up from the baseline in Italic letters.

content.text: The
chancery italic

capitals lean to the right at the same angle as the lowercase. They are more compressed than Foundational capitals, and several letters (A, H, I, M, N) are written with fewer strokes because of the connected structure of the cursive tradition from which they come.
What you need
Nib: Mitchell Roundhand 3. The Italic capitals are written at 7 nib widths cap-height, the same as the Foundational capitals, but at a 45-degree nib angle rather than 30 degrees.
Paper: cartridge, 130 gsm. Rule guide lines at baseline and cap-height. If working Italic minuscule on the same page, rule x-height lines in a lighter pencil.
Nib angle and pen hold

Hold the pen at 45 degrees from the baseline. The pen rests between index and middle fingers with the thumb supporting from behind; the hand rests on the side of the little finger. The 45-degree angle produces equal-weight diagonal strokes in both directions, which gives the Italic letter its characteristic diagonal stress.
Stroke orders for key Italic capitals
A: three strokes, as in the Foundational hand, but leaned 5 to 10 degrees to the right. The crossbar sits slightly above mid-height.

H: two strokes. Stroke 1: left stem, top to bottom, leaned to the right. Stroke 2: right stem with the crossbar attached, written as a single stroke curving from the top through the crossbar and down the right stem. This two-stroke construction is characteristic of the italic capital H.

N: two strokes. Stroke 1: left stem. Stroke 2: diagonal connecting top of left stem to bottom of right stem position, then up the right stem in a single movement. The diagonal is a thin stroke at 45 degrees.
B, D, P, R: the
arch
for these letters springs from the
springing point
about two-thirds of the way up the stem, not from the very top. This gives the Italic versions of these letters their characteristic forward lean at the bowl junction.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Italic capitals look like Foundational capitals with a slight tilt added
attrs.cause: The nib angle was not adjusted from 30 to 45 degrees when switching from Foundational to Italic.

content.text: The change from 30 to 45 degrees shifts the weight distribution of every stroke. Reset the nib angle and practise the O and H before moving to the full alphabet.

attrs.cause: The slope of 5 to 10 degrees was applied differently across the letter, with some strokes steeper than others.

content.text: Rule light slant guide lines at the chosen angle (5 degrees or 10 degrees) and confirm every stem aligns with these lines before writing. 5 degrees is more formal; 10 degrees is more casual.
